content.text: Problem decomposition is the practice of breaking a complex task into smaller, independently solvable sub-problems before executing any of them. For an LLM agent, it is the difference between attempting a hundred-step task in one shot — where errors compound and context overflows — and building a
plan tree

that the agent traverses step by step, delegating each leaf to a tool call or a focused LLM prompt. Decomposition turns an unbounded problem into a bounded one, makes parallelism explicit, and lets each sub-task be verified independently.

attrs.alt: Problem decomposition tree: complex task splits into sub-problems, then leaf tasks that map to executable tool calls

content.text: Why Decomposition Matters for Agents
A language model processing a monolithic prompt for a complex task faces several failure modes:
Context saturation: all intermediate state must fit in one conversation, so later steps degrade as the window fills
Error propagation: a mistake in an early step silently corrupts all downstream reasoning
Unverifiable steps: with everything in one response, there is no hook to validate intermediate results
No parallelism: sequential tool calls serialize work that could run concurrently

Decomposition addresses all four. Sub-problems have their own context windows; each can be verified before the next starts; independent sub-problems can run in parallel; and a wrong sub-answer can be retried without re-doing the whole task. The cost is planning overhead — decomposition itself requires LLM calls — which is only worth paying when the underlying task is genuinely complex (multiple decision points, multiple tools, or output that exceeds a few paragraphs).
Decomposition Strategies
Functional Decomposition

Split by function or capability: what kind of operation does each part require? A research task might decompose into "gather data," "synthesize findings," and "format output." Each sub-task uses a different skill — search, reasoning, templating — so functional decomposition maps cleanly onto different tools or prompts.
Sequential Decomposition

When each step depends on the output of the previous one, decompose into a linear chain. This is the simplest topology: no branching, no parallelism. Use it when dependencies are strict and the task naturally flows as a pipeline.
Parallel Decomposition
When sub-problems are independent — no data flows between them — they can run concurrently. Identifying this structure requires building a
dependency graph
(DAG) where an edge from A to B means B needs A's output. Sub-problems with no incoming edges are ready to run immediately. In Python, these map naturally to
asyncio.gather()
or
concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor
Recursive Decomposition

Apply the same decomposition logic to each sub-problem until you reach leaf tasks — units that are directly executable without further planning. The recursion terminates when a sub-problem fits within a single context window and has at most one decision point. Over-decomposing (splitting leaves further) wastes planning LLM calls; under-decomposing leaves vague steps that the executor cannot handle.
Leaf Task Criteria
A leaf task is one that can be answered by a single tool call or a single focused LLM prompt — no further breakdown needed. If a task still requires deciding
which
tool to call or
how
to structure its own sub-steps, it is not yet a leaf.
Practical leaf criteria:
The task maps 1:1 to a single function call (web_search, fetch_url, run_sql, etc.)
The expected output is a single value, document, or structured record — not a multi-section report
Solving it requires no further tool calls from within the sub-task
A human expert could answer it in one paragraph or less
Python Implementation
The following pattern models a decomposition tree explicitly, solves each leaf with a tool dispatcher, and assembles results bottom-up.
End-to-End Example

task = (
    "Research the top 3 programming languages by job postings in 2024, "
    "compare their average salaries, and write a 200-word summary."
)

# Stage 1: decompose
subtasks = decompose(task)
print("Plan:")
for t in subtasks:
    print(f"  [{t.id}] {'LEAF' if t.is_leaf else 'PLAN'} deps={t.dependencies}: {t.description[:60]}")

# Possible plan:
# [t1] LEAF deps=[]:     search("top programming languages job postings 2024")
# [t2] LEAF deps=[]:     search("average salaries Python JavaScript Java 2024")
# [t3] PLAN deps=[t1,t2]: Synthesize salary and popularity data
# [t4] PLAN deps=[t3]:   Write 200-word summary

# Stage 2: execute with parallelism (t1 and t2 run concurrently)
results = execute_plan(subtasks)

# The final task's result is the answer
final_id = subtasks[-1].id
print("\nFinal answer:")
print(results[final_id])
Decomposition in Established Frameworks
Most multi-step agent frameworks implement decomposition implicitly or explicitly:
Framework
Decomposition Mechanism
Parallelism
ReAct
Implicit — Thought steps decompose ad hoc
None (sequential loop)
ReWOO Planner
Explicit — numbered plan steps with #Ei variables
Yes (independent steps)
LangGraph
Explicit — developer-defined node graph
Yes (parallel edges)
Self-Discover
Structure JSON keys as implicit sub-steps
None by default
Custom (above)
Explicit — LLM-generated SubTask list
Yes (DAG-resolved)
Verifying Sub-Task Results
One of decomposition's key advantages is that each sub-task result can be checked before being passed downstream. This catches errors early and prevents compounding.
Common Pitfalls
Decomposing Too Early

Decomposing before understanding the task structure leads to a plan that misses dependencies or creates redundant sub-tasks. Always pass the full task description — including constraints, output format, and available tools — to the planner before asking it to decompose.
Circular Dependencies

An LLM planner can emit plans where task A depends on B and B depends on A. Always check the dependency graph for cycles before execution — the topological sort in the executor above will raise an error, which is the right behavior.
Shallow Decomposition

If leaves are still described as multi-step processes ("research X, then summarize it"), the executor will struggle. Push decomposition until each leaf description corresponds to exactly one operation. A useful heuristic: if the description contains the word "then," it is not yet a leaf.
LLM-Guided vs. Static Decomposition
Two broad approaches exist for generating the decomposition plan:
LLM-Guided (Dynamic)

The agent asks the LLM to produce the decomposition plan at runtime, based on the task description and available tools. This is the approach shown in the implementation above. It is flexible — the planner can reason about
this specific task's
structure and dependencies — but it adds LLM latency before execution begins and can produce inconsistent plan shapes across runs.
Static (Hard-coded)

For well-understood, repeating task types, the decomposition can be encoded as a fixed workflow (e.g., in a LangGraph state machine or a hardcoded function pipeline). This eliminates planning latency and is fully deterministic, but cannot adapt to novel task variations.
Hybrid: Template + Fill-in

A middle ground: define the decomposition shape statically ("always: gather → synthesize → format") but fill in sub-task specifics dynamically using the LLM. The planner only needs to parameterize the template slots, not invent the entire structure. This is the most practical approach for production systems where task categories are known ahead of time.

# Hybrid: fixed pipeline shape, dynamic parameterization

PIPELINE_TEMPLATE = [
    {"id": "gather",    "role": "Gather relevant raw data for the task"},
    {"id": "synthesize","role": "Synthesize gathered data into findings", "deps": ["gather"]},
    {"id": "format",    "role": "Format findings per output spec",         "deps": ["synthesize"]},
]

def parameterize_pipeline(task: str, template: list[dict]) -> list[SubTask]:
    """Ask the LLM to fill in concrete descriptions for each pipeline stage."""
    prompt = f"""Given this task, write a concrete description for each pipeline stage.
Task: {task}

Stages:
{json.dumps(template, indent=2)}

Return a JSON array in the same order, each object containing:
  "id" (unchanged), "description" (specific to the task), "dependencies" (from deps field),
  "is_leaf" (true if the stage maps to a single tool call).
Output only JSON."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL, max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text.strip().lstrip("```json").lstrip("```").rstrip("```").strip()
    return [SubTask(**item) for item in json.loads(raw)]
Estimating Decomposition Cost
Before adding decomposition to an agent, consider whether the planning overhead is justified:
Simple tasks (≤ 2 tool calls, single output):
skip decomposition
. A single ReAct loop is cheaper.
Medium tasks (3–8 tool calls, clear pipeline):
use a static or hybrid template
. One planning LLM call is justified.
Complex tasks (many tools, branching, long output):
invest in dynamic decomposition
. Planning cost is small relative to execution cost.

A useful rule: if the task description contains more than three verbs that describe distinct operations, decompose. If it has one or two, try a direct prompt first. Failed direct attempts are informative — they reveal exactly which sub-step needs more structure, guiding decomposition with empirical evidence rather than guesswork.

When parallelism is available, the wall-clock benefit compounds with task size. A 10-step task with 4 parallelizable steps completes in roughly 7 sequential steps' time. Track both LLM-call count and wall-clock time when benchmarking decomposition strategies on your specific workload.
Summary
Problem decomposition converts an unbounded task into a structured execution plan with verifiable steps and explicit parallelism opportunities. Applied to LLM agents, it yields:
Smaller context windows per step, avoiding saturation in long tasks
Early error detection — a bad sub-result can be retried without re-running the whole task
Parallel execution of independent sub-problems, reducing wall-clock time
A testable intermediate representation: the plan itself can be inspected and edited before execution

The right decomposition depth is where each leaf maps 1:1 to a single executable operation. Too shallow leaves ambiguity; too deep wastes planning calls. In practice, an LLM planner calibrates this correctly when given a clear list of available tools — leaves should match tool signatures.
